criteria: Allocate executable memory using mmap with PROT_READ | PROT_WRITE (excluding PROT_EXEC) for initial code writing phase
Transition memory permissions from writable to executable using mprotect(PROT_READ | PROT_EXEC) after code emission, achieving W^X compliance
Invoke __builtin___clear_cache or equivalent after mprotect on ARM64 platforms (Apple Silicon, native ARM) for instruction cache coherency
Emit mov reg/imm instructions with correct REX.W prefix (0x48) for 64-bit operand size operations
Correctly encode all 16 general-purpose registers (rax-r15) with appropriate REX.B and REX.R prefix bits for registers r8-r15
Generate ModR/M bytes for register-to-register (Mod=11), register-to-memory, and immediate operand forms
Construct callable function pointers from emitted code that execute correctly when invoked
Verify at least 5 test functions produce correct results: return constant, add two arguments, negate value, multiply two arguments, and compound arithmetic expression
Cross-validate emitted byte sequences against expected encodings using objdump -d or Capstone disassembly
Implement proper resource cleanup with munmap when destroying code buffers
Handle mmap and mprotect failures gracefully with appropriate error messages
Translate bytecode arithmetic operations (ADD, SUB, MUL, DIV) to corresponding x86-64 instructions (add, sub, imul, idiv/div) with correct REX.W prefix for 64-bit operands
Signed division correctly emits 'cqo; idiv' sequence (sign-extend rax into rdx:rax) before the division instruction
Unsigned division correctly emits 'xor rdx, rdx; div' sequence (zero rdx) before the division instruction
Implement linear scan register allocator that maps the top N stack slots to machine registers (r8-r11, rcx, rsi, rdi)
Register allocator correctly spills to the stack frame when expression depth exceeds available registers, using rbp-relative addressing
Comparison bytecodes (EQ, LT, GT, LE, GE, NE) emit cmp followed by setcc instruction, with result zero-extended to 64 bits
Forward jumps are emitted with placeholder rel8 offsets and back-patched once the target label address is known
Back-patched jump offsets are calculated relative to the END of the jump instruction (address after rel8 byte), not the beginning
JIT-compiled expressions produce bit-identical results to the interpreter for a test suite of at least 20 expressions
Test suite includes edge cases: division semantics, negative numbers, arithmetic overflow wrapping, nested operations
All emitted instructions correctly encode ModR/M bytes for register-to-register and register-to-immediate operand forms
Division by zero handling: either explicit checks emitted or documented that caller must ensure non-zero divisor
Emit function prologue that pushes rbp, saves callee-saved registers (rbx, r12-r15 as needed), and aligns rsp to 16 bytes after prologue completion
Emit function epilogue that restores callee-saved registers in reverse order, pops rbp, and returns via ret instruction
Read first 6 integer arguments from correct ABI registers (rdi, rsi, rdx, rcx, r8, r9) and access additional arguments from stack at correct offsets
Place integer return value in rax before epilogue executes
Verify 16-byte stack alignment by successfully calling printf or another C library function that uses SSE instructions without crashing
Implement JIT-to-interpreter trampoline that saves JIT register state, sets up interpreter frame, and returns result in rax
Implement interpreter-to-JIT trampoline that marshals arguments from operand stack to ABI registers and pushes return value
Allocate local variables at correct negative offsets from rbp, supporting at least 32 8-byte local variables
Compile recursive functions (factorial, fibonacci) that execute correctly to depth 100 without stack overflow or register corruption
Per-function invocation counter decrements on each interpreted call using the decrement-and-branch pattern with < 5% interpreter overhead (approximately 1 cycle per call)
When a function's counter reaches zero, the function is scheduled for JIT compilation with a guard to prevent reentrant compilation
JIT-compiled function replaces the interpreter entry point so subsequent calls execute native code directly via function pointer dispatch
Constant folding optimization evaluates compile-time constant expressions during bytecode traversal and emits folded results as single immediates
Dead store elimination identifies and skips emitting stores to local variables that are overwritten before being read (within basic blocks)
Strength reduction replaces multiply-by-power-of-2 with left-shift (shl) and handles multiply-by-0 and multiply-by-1 as special cases
Benchmark demonstrates at least 5x speedup on compute-intensive loop (sum 1 to 1M) comparing JIT execution after warmup to pure interpretation
Cold functions (below threshold) continue executing in interpreter with counter decrement overhead only, never triggering compilation
Code cache uses mmap with PROT_READ|PROT_WRITE for emission then mprotect to PROT_READ|PROT_EXEC before execution
Counter uses signed 32-bit integer starting at HOT_THRESHOLD (default 1000) with zero-check to detect hot functions
Type guards are emitted before type-specialized operations using conditional branches (jnz/jz) to deoptimization stubs when tag bits don't match expected type
Deoptimization stub saves all general-purpose registers (rax-r15) and frame pointer, then calls C deoptimization function with entry ID and register state pointer

Deoptimization function reconstructs interpreter frame by reading value mappings from side table, mapping JIT registers and spill slots to interpreter operand stack and local variables in correct order
Guard failure counter increments on each deoptimization event, with per-guard tracking for profiling which assumptions fail most frequently
Functions accumulating MAX_DEOPT_BEFORE_BLACKLIST (default 3) deoptimizations are marked blacklisted and their JIT entries are invalidated
Test case demonstrates: warm up function with integers → JIT compiles → call with non-integer → guard fails → deoptimization stub runs → interpreter produces correct result
Invalidated JIT code is added to zombie list and freed only after stack scan confirms no active frames reference the code
State mapping side table records register-to-bytecode-slot mappings at each guard point, capturing operand stack depth and local variable locations
Deoptimization resumes interpreter execution at the exact bytecode instruction where the failed guard was placed, with reconstructed VM state matching interpreter expectations
No memory leaks: zombie code blocks are periodically garbage collected, deoptimization frames are freed after use, and side tables are freed when code is invalidated
Type guards emit a runtime check before type-specialized operations that branches to a deoptimization stub on failure
Deoptimization stub saves current machine state (registers, stack), reconstructs interpreter VM state (instruction pointer, operand stack, local variables), and transfers control to interpreter
After deoptimization, interpreter continues execution from exact bytecode instruction corresponding to failed guard with correct VM state
Guard failure counter tracks frequency of each guard failure with functions exceeding threshold blacklisted from JIT compilation
Test demonstrates JIT compiles function assuming integer arguments, receives non-integer argument, triggers deoptimization, and interpreter produces correct result
No memory leaks from deoptimized code: invalidated native code buffers are freed or reused after all active frames exit the deoptimized code
Side table maps every JIT register and stack slot back to corresponding bytecode local variable or operand stack entry
Deoptimization correctly handles operand stack ordering (bottom-to-top mapping when reconstructing interpreter state)
Allocate memory using mmap with PROT_READ|PROT_WRITE (no PROT_EXEC) for code writing
After code emission is complete, change page permissions to PROT_READ|PROT_EXEC using mprotect, removing write permission (W^X compliance)
On platforms requiring it (macOS ARM64 via Rosetta, native ARM), flush the instruction cache after permission change using __builtin___clear_cache or equivalent
Emit basic x86-64 instructions (mov reg/imm, add, sub, imul, ret) as correctly encoded byte sequences with REX prefix for 64-bit operands
Support all 16 general-purpose registers (rax-r15) with correct REX.B/REX.R encoding for registers r8-r15
ModR/M byte encoding correctly handles register-to-register, register-to-memory, and immediate operand forms
Generated code is callable as a C function pointer and returns correct results for at least 5 test cases (e.g., return 42, add two arguments, negate a value)
Emitted bytes match expected encoding verified by disassembling output with a disassembler (objdump -d or Capstone)
Translate bytecode arithmetic operations (ADD, SUB, MUL, DIV) to corresponding x86-64 instructions (add, sub, imul, idiv/div)
Signed division correctly emits 'cqo; idiv' (sign-extend rax into rdx:rax); unsigned division emits 'xor rdx, rdx; div'
Simple register allocator maps the top N stack slots to machine registers and spills to the stack frame when expression depth exceeds available registers
Comparison bytecodes (EQ, LT, GT, etc.) emit cmp followed by conditional set (setcc) or conditional jump (jcc) instructions
Forward jumps are emitted with placeholder offsets and back-patched once the target address is known. Back-patching produces correct relative offsets
JIT-compiled expressions produce bit-identical results to the interpreter for a test suite of at least 20 expressions including edge cases (division by zero, integer overflow, nested operations)
Generated function prologue pushes rbp, sets up stack frame, saves all callee-saved registers used (rbx, r12-r15, rbp), and aligns rsp to 16 bytes
Generated function epilogue restores callee-saved registers, tears down the stack frame, and returns via ret
Arguments are read from the correct registers (rdi, rsi, rdx, rcx, r8, r9 for the first 6 integer args) and from the stack for additional arguments, per System V AMD64 ABI
Return values are placed in rax (integer) per ABI convention
Stack is 16-byte aligned immediately before every call instruction, verified by a test that calls a C library function (e.g., printf) from JIT-compiled code without crashing
Trampoline mechanism allows JIT-compiled code to call interpreter functions and vice versa, correctly saving/restoring VM state across the transition
Local variables are allocated in the stack frame with correct offsets, supporting at least 32 local variables
Recursive JIT-compiled functions work correctly for at least depth 100 (e.g., factorial, fibonacci)
Per-function invocation counter is incremented on each interpreted call with minimal overhead (< 5% interpreter slowdown)
When a function's counter exceeds a configurable threshold (default: 1000), it is scheduled for JIT compilation
JIT compilation replaces the function's dispatch entry so subsequent calls execute native code directly without interpreter overhead

Basic optimization passes during JIT compilation: constant folding (evaluate compile-time constants), dead store elimination (remove unused assignments), and strength reduction (replace multiply by power-of-2 with shift)
Benchmark demonstrates at least 5x speedup on a compute-intensive loop (e.g., sum 1 to 1M) when JIT-compiled vs. interpreted
Cold functions (below threshold) continue to execute in the interpreter with negligible profiling overhead
Emit_type_guard_with_test_and_jnz_instruction
Create_DeoptEntry_with_state_snapshot_for_each_guard
Track_value_locations_in_StateTracker_during_compilation
Snapshot_includes_all_operand_stack_and_local_variable_values
Emit_deopt_stub_that_saves_all_registers
Call_C_deoptimize_function_from_stub
Reconstruct_interpreter_frame_from_saved_JIT_state
Sort_operand_stack_values_by_sort_order_before_pushing
Set_interpreter_PC_to_bytecode_pc_from_DeoptEntry
Record_guard_failure_in_DeoptProfile_with_atomic_increment
Blacklist_function_after_MAX_DEOPT_BEFORE_BLACKLIST_failures
Add_invalidated_code_to_zombie_list
Scan_stack_for_return_addresses_in_zombie_gc
Free_zombie_code_only_when_no_stack_references_exist
Link_guard_jumps_to_stub_addresses_with_rel32_patching
Verify_bit_identical_results_between_JIT_and_interpreter
Guard_overhead_less_than_3_cycles_when_correctly_predicted
Deoptimization_time_less_than_100_microseconds
Side_table_size_less_than_20_percent_of_code_size
